front: What are the indications for a bone scan in prostate cancer?
back: PSA > 10 ng/mL, Gleason score > 7, or bony pain.

subject: Surgery
front: What Gleason score defines the low risk group for prostate cancer?

subject: Surgery
front: What Gleason score defines the intermediate favourable risk group?

subject: Surgery
front: What Gleason score defines the intermediate unfavourable risk group?

subject: Surgery
front: What Gleason score defines the high risk group?

subject: Surgery
front: For localised prostate cancer (T1, T2a) in patients < 70 years with life expectancy > 10 years and aggressive tumours, what is the management?
back: Radical prostatectomy, removing the prostate, iliac and obturator lymph nodes, and seminal vesicles.

subject: Surgery
front: For localised prostate cancer in patients > 70 years with life expectancy < 10 years and low-grade tumours, what is the management?
back: Observation.

subject: Surgery
front: For locally advanced prostate cancer (T2b, T3, T4) in patients < 70 years with life expectancy > 10 years, what is the management?
back: Brachytherapy (radiotherapy); if residual disease remains, radical prostatectomy is performed; if not, surveillance.

subject: Surgery
front: For locally advanced prostate cancer in patients > 70 years with life expectancy < 10 years and G1/G2 tumours, what is the management?
back: Brachytherapy plus androgen deprivation therapy (ADT).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the first-line management for metastatic prostate cancer?
back: Androgen deprivation therapy (ADT).

subject: Surgery
front: What medical options are used for androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)?
back: LHRH (GnRH) agonists (goserelin, buserelin) plus antiandrogens (flutamide, abiraterone).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgical option for androgen deprivation therapy?
back: Bilateral orchidectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What treatment options exist for hormone-resistant prostate cancer?
back: Chemotherapy, radiotherapy, Sipuleucel-T (a T-cell vaccine), and Radium-223 (alpha rays) for bony metastases.

subject: Surgery
front: What does the French (Fr) size of a Foley's catheter measure?
back: The outer circumference (2*pi*r).

subject: Surgery
front: What Foley's catheter size corresponds to the colours white, green, orange, and red?
back: White: 12 Fr. Green: 14 Fr. Orange: 16 Fr. Red: 18 Fr.

subject: Surgery
front: How long can a rubber Foley's catheter be left in place, versus a silicone Foley's catheter?
back: Rubber: 25-30 days. Silicone: 90 days (3 months).

subject: Surgery
front: How is a 'stuck' Foley's catheter managed?
back: USG-guided suprapubic puncture.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a 3-way Foley's catheter used for, and what does it consist of?
back: Used in clot retention (post-TURP); has 3 channels - a balloon port, a urine drainage port, and an irrigation port.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of bladder rupture, and what causes it?
back: Extraperitoneal rupture; secondary to pelvic fracture.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of extraperitoneal bladder rupture?
back: Deep perineal haematoma and inability to pass urine.

subject: Surgery
front: What causes intraperitoneal bladder rupture?
back: Blunt or penetrating trauma to a full bladder.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of intraperitoneal bladder rupture?
back: Peritonitis and syncopal attack.

subject: Surgery
front: What investigations are used for bladder trauma?
back: CT urography (if stable) and MCU (micturating cystourethrogram).

subject: Surgery
front: What does MCU show in intraperitoneal bladder rupture?
back: Spillage of contrast into the peritoneal cavity.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of extraperitoneal bladder rupture?
back: Foley's or suprapubic catheter (SPC) for 7 days.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of intraperitoneal bladder rupture?
back: Laparotomy with bladder repair in 2 layers, plus Foley's/SPC.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of bladder cancer overall, and its risk factors (the '3 Cs')?
back: Transitional cell carcinoma; risk factors are chemicals, cyclophosphamide, and cigarettes.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of bladder cancer in Africa, and its risk factors (the '2 Ss')?
back: Squamous cell carcinoma; risk factors are smoking and schistosomiasis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the classic clinical feature of bladder cancer?
back: Gross haematuria.

subject: Surgery
front: What investigations are used for bladder cancer?
back: USG KUB (cannot differentiate clots from growth), urine cytology, and cystoscopic biopsy (TURBT).

subject: Surgery
front: What does Ta represent in bladder cancer staging?
back: Non-invasive papillary carcinoma.

subject: Surgery
front: What does Tis represent in bladder cancer staging?
back: Carcinoma in situ.

subject: Surgery
front: What does T1 represent in bladder cancer staging?
back: Tumour above the muscle layer.

subject: Surgery
front: What does T2 represent in bladder cancer staging?
back: Tumour invading the muscle layer.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of pTa bladder cancer?
back: Observation, or a single dose of intravesical chemotherapy to suppress field cancerisation.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of pTis bladder cancer?
back: Intravesical BCG (immunotherapy).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of pT1 bladder cancer?
back: Intravesical chemotherapy or BCG.

subject: Surgery
front: How often should check cystoscopy be performed after treatment of non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer, and why?
back: Every 3 months, to look for recurrence.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of T2 (muscle-invasive) bladder cancer?
back: Surgery (radical cystectomy).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of T3/T4 bladder cancer?
back: Chemotherapy followed by surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What structures are removed in radical cystectomy?
back: The bladder, iliac lymph nodes, obturator lymph nodes, and urethra.

subject: Surgery
front: When is partial cystectomy used for bladder cancer?
back: For small tumours restricted to the dome of the bladder, not involving the ureteric orifice.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most commonly used method of urinary diversion after cystectomy?
back: Ileal conduit (ureters anastomosed to an ileal segment brought out as an ileostomy).

subject: Surgery
front: Why is ureterosigmoid anastomosis now considered obsolete for urinary diversion?
back: Due to an increased risk of cancer (100-fold), recurrent UTI, and hyperchloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic acidosis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the approximate length of the male urethra?

subject: Surgery
front: What is hypospadias, and what is its rank among congenital urogenital anomalies?
back: The most common congenital urogenital anomaly; involves a ventrally placed urethral opening.

subject: Surgery
front: What conditions is hypospadias associated with?
back: Micropenis and undescended testis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common and mildest type of hypospadias, and the most severe type?
back: Glanular type is most common and mildest; perineal type is most severe.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of hypospadias?
back: Hooded prepuce, downward-directed urine stream, downward bending of the penis (chordee), and difficulty during intercourse.

subject: Surgery
front: At what age is hypospadias surgery typically performed?
back: Between 6 to 12 months of age.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the three steps of hypospadias surgery?
back: Orthoplasty (chordee correction), urethroplasty (repositioning of the urethra), and glanuloplasty (skin cover).

subject: Surgery
front: When are single-stage vs double-staged procedures used for hypospadias repair?
back: Single-stage: distal and mild hypospadias. Double-staged (e.g. Thiersch-Duplay, Dennis-Browne): proximal hypospadias.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is circumcision contraindicated in hypospadias patients before repair?
back: Because the foreskin is needed for reconstruction.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the complications of hypospadias surgery?
back: Infection, meatal stenosis, and urethrocutaneous fistula.

subject: Surgery
front: What is ectopia vesicae (bladder exstrophy)?
back: A deficiency of the anterior abdominal wall and anterior bladder wall below the umbilicus.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of ectopia vesicae?
back: Urine dribbling from the bladder, undescended testis, bifid clitoris, and pubic diastasis.

subject: Surgery
front: What causes anterior urethral injury, and what type of haematoma results?
back: Direct trauma or straddle injury; causes a superficial perineal/butterfly haematoma around the penis and scrotum.

subject: Surgery
front: What causes posterior urethral injury, and what are its clinical features?
back: Secondary to pelvic fracture; causes a deep perineal haematoma, a positive Vermooten sign (floating prostate), blood at the tip of the meatus, and inability to pass urine.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, straddle injury typically causes injury to which part of the urethra?
back: The anterior urethra.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, pelvic fracture typically causes injury to which part of the urethra?
back: The posterior urethra.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what is the most common site of urethral rupture?
back: The bulbo-membranous junction.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what are the classic clinical features of urethral trauma?
back: Blood at the meatus and inability to void.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation of choice for urethral trauma?
back: Retrograde urethrogram (RGU).

subject: Surgery
front: How is suspected urethral trauma managed if the bladder is full?
back: Suprapubic catheterisation (SPC).

subject: Surgery
front: How is suspected urethral trauma managed if the bladder is not full?
back: Wait for the bladder to fill, then attempt one trial of micturition; if this fails, manage as a urethral stricture.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, if the urinary bladder is not palpable, what is the management approach for suspected urethral injury?
back: Wait and watch.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, if RGU is positive for urethral injury, what is the initial management, and what definitive procedure follows and after how long?
back: Suprapubic catheterisation (SPC) initially, followed by delayed urethroplasty (using a buccal mucosa graft) after 4-6 weeks.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a short, incomplete urethral stricture managed?
back: OIU/VIU (optical/visual internal urethrotomy).

subject: Surgery
front: How is a short, complete urethral stricture managed?
back: Excision with end-to-end anastomosis.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a long, complete urethral stricture managed?
back: Excision with urethroplasty using a buccal mucosal graft.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a fracture of the shaft of the penis, and in what setting does it typically occur?
back: A tear in the corpora, typically occurring during intercourse when the penis is erect.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of penile fracture?
back: A popping sound followed by pain, and eggplant deformity of the penis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of penile fracture?
back: Haematoma evacuation and repair of the corpora.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a posterior urethral valve, and what is its mechanism of obstruction?
back: A one-way valve in the posterior urethra.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Young's classification Type 1 posterior urethral valve, and how common is it?
back: The most common type; located anteroinferior to the verumontanum.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of posterior urethral valve?
back: A male child with recurrent UTI.

subject: Surgery
front: What investigations are used for posterior urethral valve?
back: MCU and USG.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of posterior urethral valve?
back: Fulguration of the valve.

subject: Surgery
front: What is phimosis?
back: Inability to retract the foreskin.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of symptomatic phimosis?
back: Ballooning of the foreskin, UTIs, and difficult intercourse.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of phimosis?
back: Circumcision.

subject: Surgery
front: What is paraphimosis?
back: Failure to reposit the foreskin after catheterisation, causing a constriction ring around the penis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of paraphimosis?
back: Conservative reduction first; if this fails, dorsal slit surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Peyronie's disease, and what does it cause?
back: Calcific deposition in the corpora, causing the penis to bend towards the side of the plaque.

subject: Surgery
front: How is Peyronie's disease diagnosed?
back: Clinical examination plus MRI.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the medical management of Peyronie's disease?
back: Intralesional collagenase clostridium histolyticum injection.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgical management of Peyronie's disease if medical management fails?
back: Nesbit's surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What is priapism, and after what duration does ischaemia/necrosis of the penis occur?
back: A prolonged erection lasting more than 4 hours; ischaemia/necrosis develops after 6 hours.

subject: Surgery
front: What is high-flow priapism, and what is its cause?
back: Caused by increased blood flow into the penis; usually secondary to trauma; it is painless with oxygenated blood on penile ABG.

subject: Surgery
front: What is low-flow priapism, and what are its common causes?
back: The most common type, caused by venous obstruction; causes include spinal injury, papaverine injection, and in children, leukaemia or sickle cell anaemia; it is painful with deoxygenated blood on penile ABG.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation of choice for priapism?
back: Penile angiography.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of priapism?
back: Sedate the patient, then inject adrenaline into the corpora; if this fails, shunt surgery (Greyhack shunt/corporo-saphenous shunt).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the histology of penile cancer?
back: Squamous cell carcinoma.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the premalignant conditions of penile cancer?
back: Bowen's disease (reddish papules on the shaft), erythroplasia of Queyrat (reddish papules on the glans), balanitis xerotica obliterans, genital warts (HPV), and leukoplakia.

subject: Surgery
front: What does Jackson staging T1 represent in penile cancer?
back: Skin involvement.

subject: Surgery
front: What does Jackson staging T2 represent?
back: Corpora involvement.

subject: Surgery
front: What does Jackson staging T3 represent?
back: Urethral involvement.

subject: Surgery
front: What does Jackson staging T4 represent?
back: Involvement of adjacent structures.

subject: Surgery
front: What investigations are used for penile cancer?
back: Biopsy of the lesion for diagnosis, and PET-CT to rule out spread.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of in-situ penile cancer lesions?
back: Topical 5-FU cream.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of distally placed penile cancer, and what margin is used?
back: Partial penectomy, with a 0.5 cm margin, provided the residual stump is >= 2 cm.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of proximally placed penile cancer?
back: Total amputation.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of penile cancer if lymph nodes are not involved?
back: Sentinel lymph node biopsy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of penile cancer if lymph nodes are involved?
back: Ilioinguinal lymph node dissection or radiotherapy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of death in penile cancer?
back: Erosion of the femoral/iliac vessels, due to involved lymph nodes.

subject: Surgery
front: What triggers normal testicular descent?
back: Differential growth of the abdominal wall, hormonal factors, and the pull of the gubernaculum.

subject: Surgery
front: At what age does normal testicular descent begin, and through what stages does it pass?
back: Begins at 3 months; passes through the iliac fossa (6 months), inguinal canal (7 months), superficial ring (8 months), to the scrotum (9 months).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common site of an undescended testis, and which side is more commonly affected?
back: The inguinal canal; the right side is more commonly affected than the left.

subject: Surgery
front: What is cryptorchidism?
back: Bilateral undescended testis.

subject: Surgery
front: What complications can result from undescended testis?
back: Trauma, epididymo-orchitis, sterility/infertility, torsion, indirect inguinal hernia (most common), and seminoma.

subject: Surgery
front: How does infertility risk differ between bilateral and unilateral undescended testis, even after orchidopexy?
back: Bilateral: decreased fertility persists even after orchidopexy. Unilateral: infertility rates are similar to the general population.

subject: Surgery
front: What histological changes occur in an undescended testis?
back: Decreased testicular volume, and increased risk of intratubular germ cell neoplasia (0-7 times increased risk of seminoma).

subject: Surgery
front: How are Sertoli cells vs Leydig cells affected in undescended testis?
back: Sertoli cells are more affected, leading to infertility; Leydig cells are less affected, so secondary sexual characteristics remain normal.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common malignancy arising in an undescended testis?
back: Seminoma.

subject: Surgery
front: How does the risk of malignancy differ if orchidopexy is performed before vs after puberty?
back: Before puberty: 2-3 times increased risk. After puberty: 5-6 times increased risk.

subject: Surgery
front: At what age is orchidopexy surgery best performed for undescended testis?
back: 6-12 months of age (spontaneous descent may occur up to 5 months).

subject: Surgery
front: What is an ectopic testis, and what is its most common site?
back: A testis deviated from the normal path of descent; most commonly found in the superficial inguinal pouch; managed with orchidopexy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a retractile testis, and how is it managed?
back: A normal variant where the testis is in the scrotum but occasionally retracts into the inguinal canal; managed with reassurance only.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the risk factors for testicular torsion?
back: Testicular inversion, undescended testis, torsion of the appendix testis (Morgagni cyst, causing the blue dot sign), and Bell Clapper deformity (high investment of the tunica vaginalis, the most important risk factor).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of testicular torsion, and its key differential diagnosis?
back: Scrotal pain and swelling; the key differential is epididymo-orchitis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Prehn's sign, and how does it differ between torsion and epididymo-orchitis?
back: Lifting the testis; pain decreases in epididymo-orchitis, but pain increases in torsion.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Deming's sign in testicular torsion?
back: The affected testis lies at a higher level.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Angel sign in testicular torsion?
back: A transversely placed testis.

subject: Surgery
front: What degree of twist causes rapid ischemia in testicular torsion?
back: 720 degrees (double twist).

subject: Surgery
front: What investigation confirms the diagnosis of testicular torsion?
back: Doppler ultrasound.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of testicular torsion, and what is the salvage rate based on timing?
back: Scrotal exploration with untwisting; salvage rate is approximately 100% if done within 6 hours, but less than 20% if done after 24 hours.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a hydrocele, and what is the most common type?
back: Accumulation of fluid in the tunica vaginalis; vaginal hydrocele is the most common type.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a primary vaginal hydrocele, and its clinical features?
back: The most common type, caused by decreased absorption; presents as a tense swelling, with the testis not palpable separately, and is brilliantly transilluminant.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a secondary vaginal hydrocele, and its clinical features?
back: Caused by increased secretion secondary to infections, trauma, or tumours; presents as a lax swelling with the testis palpable.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of congenital hydrocele?
back: Herniotomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What surgical procedure is used for a smaller hydrocele sac, and a larger sac?
back: Smaller sac: Lord's plication. Larger sac: Jaboulay's procedure (eversion of the sac).

subject: Surgery
front: What is a hydrocele of the cord, and what is its underlying anatomical basis?
back: Related to a patent processus vaginalis, similar to the basis of congenital inguinal hernia.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a varicocele, and which side is more commonly affected?
back: Dilated, tortuous veins of the pampiniform plexus; left side is more commonly affected than right.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is the left side more commonly affected by varicocele?
back: The left testicular vein is longer, opens at right angles into the left renal vein, and can be compressed by the sigmoid colon.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of varicocele?
back: Mostly asymptomatic, but can cause infertility; on examination, dilated veins have a 'bag of worms' consistency.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation of choice for varicocele?
back: Doppler ultrasound.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the first-line treatment for varicocele?
back: Percutaneous embolisation.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most effective surgical treatment for varicocele?
back: Microsurgical varicocelectomy (surgical ligation).

subject: Surgery
front: Why does varicocele have a high recurrence rate after treatment?
back: Due to the dual blood supply of the testis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Fournier's gangrene?
back: Necrotising fasciitis of the perineal region, caused by synergistic aerobic and anaerobic bacteria.

subject: Surgery
front: In whom is Fournier's gangrene most commonly seen?
back: Immunocompromised patients, particularly those with diabetes mellitus.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is the testis typically spared in Fournier's gangrene?
back: Due to its dual blood supply.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of Fournier's gangrene?
back: Pain, fever, and sepsis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most important step in managing Fournier's gangrene?
back: Aggressive debridement, along with broad-spectrum antibiotics.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Meleney's gangrene, in the context of Fournier's gangrene?
back: Gangrene that extends into the abdominal wall.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common testicular tumour in children?
back: Yolk sac tumour.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common testicular tumour overall?
back: Seminoma.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common testicular tumour in the elderly?
back: Lymphoma.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of testicular tumours?
back: Painless testicular mass; abdominal lump (from para-aortic lymph node involvement, the first draining nodes).

subject: Surgery
front: What testicular tumour causes precocious puberty and masculinisation?
back: Leydig cell tumour.

subject: Surgery
front: What testicular tumour causes feminisation?
back: Sertoli cell tumour.

subject: Surgery
front: What tumour markers are used for testicular tumours?
back: AFP, beta-hCG, LDH, and PLAP (increased in seminoma, though not included in TNM staging).

subject: Surgery
front: What does pT1 represent in testicular tumour staging?
back: Involves testis and epididymis without lymphovascular invasion.

subject: Surgery
front: What does pT2 represent in testicular tumour staging?
back: With lymphovascular invasion.

subject: Surgery
front: What does pT3 represent in testicular tumour staging?
back: Invades the spermatic cord.

subject: Surgery
front: What does pT4 represent in testicular tumour staging?
back: Invades the scrotum.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Chevassu manoeuvre, and its role in testicular tumour diagnosis?
back: A technique used in suspected cases; if cancer is confirmed, high inguinal orchidectomy is performed via a high inguinal incision to deliver the testis.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is transscrotal biopsy avoided in suspected testicular tumours?
back: Due to increased risk of upstaging the tumour.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the characteristic imaging finding of metastatic seminoma?
back: 'Cannonball' metastases (in the lungs).

subject: Surgery
front: What chemotherapy regimen (BEP) is used for testicular tumours, and what does it stand for?
back: Bleomycin, Etoposide, and Cisplatin.

subject: Surgery
front: What does RPLND stand for in testicular tumour management?
back: Retroperitoneal Lymph Node Dissection.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of Stage I seminoma vs Stage I non-seminomatous tumours?
back: Seminoma: one cycle of carboplatin, with or without radiotherapy. Non-seminomatous: one cycle of BEP chemotherapy plus RPLND.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of Stage II seminoma vs non-seminomatous tumours?
back: Seminoma: BEP chemotherapy. Non-seminomatous: BEP chemotherapy plus RPLND.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of Stage III and IV seminoma and non-seminomatous tumours?
back: BEP chemotherapy; if residual disease remains, radiotherapy or RPLND is performed.

subject: Surgery
front: What information should be gathered from the driver/responder at a trauma scene?
back: Mechanism of injury, injuries sustained, signs and symptoms, and treatment already given.

subject: Surgery
front: What information should be gathered from the patient in trauma (per Marrow), matching the AMPLE mnemonic?
back: Allergies, medications, past history, last meal, and events leading to trauma.

subject: Surgery
front: How is complete C-spine immobilization achieved in a trauma patient?
back: Using 3 straps and a rigid board, covering the head/neck, thorax, and pelvis, combined with manual in-line stabilization.

subject: Surgery
front: How many people are required to remove a helmet from a trauma patient, and why?
back: 2 people, to stabilise the C-spine during removal.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the three peaks of the trimodal distribution of mortality in trauma?
back: At impact (due to severe injuries), within 1 hour (the 'golden hour', due to airway/breathing/circulatory causes), and days to weeks later (due to sepsis or delayed complications).

subject: Surgery
front: What causes death in the 'at impact' phase of trauma?
back: Severe head injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What causes death within the golden hour (1 hour) of trauma?
back: Airway obstruction or tracheobronchial tree obstruction; tension or open pneumothorax; massive haemothorax, cardiac tamponade, or acute circulatory arrest.

subject: Surgery
front: What causes death in the delayed (days to weeks) phase of trauma?
back: Delayed head injury complications or sepsis.

subject: Surgery
front: How can mortality be reduced in trauma, per the golden hour concept?
back: By providing proper care within the first hour after injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What is triage, and on what basis is it performed?
back: Sorting patients based on treatment priority.

subject: Surgery
front: What does the P1 (red) priority category represent, and its management timeframe?
back: Emergency/immediate priority, for life-threatening injuries; managed within 1 hour.

subject: Surgery
front: What does the P2 (yellow) priority category represent?
back: Urgent priority, including fractures; the patient is stabilised first, and definitive treatment can wait.

subject: Surgery
front: What does the P3 (green) priority category represent?
back: Delayed priority - 'walking wounded' patients with minor bruises/lacerations; managed with first aid.

subject: Surgery
front: What does the P4 (blue) priority category represent?
back: Expectant priority - moribund patients, managed with supportive/palliative care (pain relief).

subject: Surgery
front: What does the black priority category represent in triage?
back: Dead bodies.

subject: Surgery
topic: ATLS: Primary Survey, Secondary Survey & C-Spine
front: What is the goal of the primary survey in ATLS, and what does it assess?
back: Stabilizing life-threatening injuries, assessed via ABCD.

subject: Surgery
topic: ATLS: Primary Survey, Secondary Survey & C-Spine
front: What is the goal of the secondary survey in ATLS?
back: A detailed examination to look for other injuries, performed after the primary survey.

subject: Surgery
topic: ATLS: Primary Survey, Secondary Survey & C-Spine
front: What are the NEXUS criteria used for in cervical spine assessment?
back: Determining whether cervical spine imaging is needed; criteria include neurological deficit, alcohol/intoxication, extreme distracting injuries, inability to provide history (altered sensorium), and midline spinal tenderness.

subject: Surgery
topic: ATLS: Primary Survey, Secondary Survey & C-Spine
front: What is done for C-spine protection if any NEXUS criteria are positive?
back: Imaging of the cervical spine plus a cervical collar, to prevent further injury.

subject: Surgery
topic: ATLS: Primary Survey, Secondary Survey & C-Spine
front: What technique is used to examine the back of a trauma patient, and how many people are required?
back: The log roll technique; requires 4 people.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the danger signs indicating a compromised airway in trauma?
back: Inability to speak, GCS <= 8, coma, and severe maxillofacial injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the emergency management for airway obstruction in trauma, and how long is it effective?
back: Needle cricothyroidotomy; it is a 2-3 minute procedure providing about 20-30 minutes of useful ventilation.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the definitive management of airway obstruction if needle cricothyroidotomy fails or more time is available?
back: Tracheostomy, or securing the airway via orotracheal/nasotracheal intubation.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is nasotracheal intubation contraindicated in suspected base of skull fracture?
back: Due to risk of cribriform plate fracture, and further injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What assessments are done for 'Breathing' in the primary survey?
back: Chest examination, pulse oximetry (as an adjunct), and imaging with a chest X-ray (AP view) and eFAST scan.

subject: Surgery
front: Is CT scan part of the primary survey in ATLS?
back: No, CT scan is not part of the primary survey.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the minimum IV access requirement for circulation management in trauma?
back: At least 2 IV cannulas, 18 gauge, each capable of delivering 1 litre of fluid.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the emergency methods for gaining venous access if IV line insertion fails?
back: Intraosseous access (needle inserted just below the tibial tuberosity), or venous cut-down (performed in the great saphenous vein, anterior to the medial malleolus).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the definitive method of venous access in trauma, and the most common insertion site?
back: A central line; the most common insertion site is the internal jugular vein.

subject: Surgery
front: According to the CRASH-2 trial, what shock parameters (SBP or HR) indicate the need for tranexamic acid?
back: Systolic BP < 90 mmHg, or heart rate > 110 bpm.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the tranexamic acid dosing protocol per the CRASH-2 trial?
back: A 1 gram infusion given over 10 minutes, followed by a 1 gram infusion given over 8 hours, leading to decreased mortality.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a pelvic binder used for in trauma?
back: Stabilizing a suspected pelvic fracture; a bed sheet may be tied around the pelvis until fracture is ruled out.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the components and scores of the eye opening response in GCS?
back: Spontaneous (4), to sound (3), to pressure (2), no response (1); cannot be tested (NT).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the components and scores of the verbal response in GCS?
back: Oriented (5), confused (4), inappropriate words (3), incomprehensible sounds (2), no response (1); cannot be tested (NT) if intubated.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the components and scores of the best motor response in GCS?
back: Obeys commands (6), localising pain (5), normal flexion/withdrawal (4), abnormal flexion/decorticate rigidity (3), abnormal extension/decerebrate rigidity (2), no response (1).

subject: Surgery
front: What GCS range defines mild, moderate, and severe head injury?
back: Mild: 13-15. Moderate: 9-12. Severe: <= 8.

subject: Surgery
front: How is the GCS-P score calculated, and what does it assess?
back: GCS-P = GCS minus the Pupil Reactivity Score (PRS); used to assess head injury severity.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the components and scores of the pupil reactivity score (PRS)?
back: Both pupils reactive to light (0), one pupil reactive (1), neither pupil reactive (2); this is subtracted from the calculated GCS.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most commonly injured organ overall and in blunt abdominal trauma?
back: The spleen.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most commonly injured organ in penetrating abdominal trauma?
back: The liver.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most commonly injured organ in gunshot wounds?
back: The small intestine.

subject: Surgery
front: What structure is most commonly injured in seat belt syndrome?
back: The mesentery.

subject: Surgery
front: What structure is most commonly injured in deceleration injury?
back: The duodeno-jejunal (DJ) flexure.

subject: Surgery
front: What does FAST (Focused Assessment Sonogram in Trauma) detect, and where are the probe placement sites?
back: Detects free fluid in the pericardium and abdomen; probe placement sites are the epigastrium, right upper quadrant, left upper quadrant, and suprapubic region.

subject: Surgery
front: What additional views are added in eFAST (extended FAST) compared to standard FAST?
back: Right and left anterior thoracic views, to assess the thoracic cavity.

subject: Surgery
front: In blunt abdominal trauma, what is the investigation of choice if the patient is haemodynamically stable vs unstable?
back: Stable: CECT. Unstable: FAST.

subject: Surgery
front: In penetrating abdominal trauma with a wound superficial to the peritoneum, what is the management?
back: Local exploration and suturing.

subject: Surgery
front: In penetrating abdominal trauma without signs of peritoneal breach, what is the investigation of choice?
back: CECT.

subject: Surgery
front: What signs suggest peritoneal breach in penetrating abdominal trauma, and what is the management?
back: Visible omentum, peritonitis, bile-stained dressing, or haemodynamic instability; managed with laparotomy.

subject: Surgery
front: Why should a penetrating object be removed only in the operating theatre?
back: Due to increased risk of bleeding upon removal.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the disadvantages of FAST scan in trauma assessment?
back: Cannot detect fluid volumes less than 100 cc, cannot detect hollow viscus injury, and is not reliable for penetrating trauma or retroperitoneal injury assessment.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines Grade I splenic injury (AAST), and its management?
back: Subcapsular haematoma < 10% surface area, or parenchymal laceration < 1 cm; managed conservatively (monitor vitals, haematocrit, serial CECT) if stable.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines Grade II splenic injury, and its management?
back: Subcapsular haematoma 10-50% surface area, intraparenchymal haematoma < 5 cm, or parenchymal laceration 1-3 cm; managed similarly to Grade I if stable.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines Grade III splenic injury, and its management?
back: Subcapsular haematoma > 50% surface area, intraparenchymal haematoma >= 5 cm, or parenchymal laceration > 3 cm; managed like Grade I/II if stable, or like Grade IV/V if unstable.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines Grade IV splenic injury, and its management?
back: Hilar vascular injury involving splenic vessels; the patient is unstable (investigation of choice: FAST); managed with splenectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines Grade V splenic injury?
back: A shattered spleen.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a Grade I-III splenic injury managed if conservative management fails or the patient is unstable?
back: Angioembolisation, followed by conservative surgery/splenic preservation if that fails.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines Grade IV liver injury (parenchymal laceration extent)?
back: Parenchymal laceration involving 25-75% of a hepatic lobe, or 1-3 Couinaud segments.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines Grade V liver injury?
back: Parenchymal laceration involving more than 75% of a hepatic lobe, or venous injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common complication after splenectomy?
back: Pulmonary complications, particularly left-sided atelectasis (besides haemorrhage and pancreatic injury).

subject: Surgery
front: What haematological changes occur after splenectomy, and are they transient or permanent?
back: Transient (about 2 weeks): increased WBC, RBC, and platelet count. Permanent: basophilic stippling, Howell-Jolly bodies, reticulocytes, and hypersegmented WBCs.

subject: Surgery
front: What is OPSI (Overwhelming Post-Splenectomy Infection), and what organisms most commonly cause it?
back: A serious post-splenectomy infection; most commonly caused by encapsulated bacteria - Pneumococcus, meningococcus, and H. influenzae.

subject: Surgery
front: In whom is OPSI more common, and within what timeframe does it typically occur?
back: More common in children than adults; occurs within the first 2 years after splenectomy, more often when splenectomy was for haematological conditions than for trauma.

subject: Surgery
front: How is OPSI prevented, and when should vaccines be given relative to elective vs emergency splenectomy?
back: Prevented with vaccines; given 2 weeks prior to elective splenectomy, or on post-op day 2/3 for emergency splenectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Pringle's manoeuvre, and where is it performed?
back: Compression of the portal triad at the foramen of Winslow, to control liver bleeding.

subject: Surgery
front: What structures make up the portal triad, compressed during Pringle's manoeuvre?
back: The hepatic artery, common bile duct, and portal vein.

subject: Surgery
front: How does the response to Pringle's manoeuvre help identify the source of liver bleeding?
back: If bleeding decreases, the source is the portal vein or hepatic artery; if bleeding continues, the source is the hepatic veins.

subject: Surgery
front: How can bleeding be controlled during liver trauma surgery using packing?
back: Using mops/gauze packing, which creates a tamponading effect.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of stable liver trauma?
back: Conservative management; if it remains stable, manage any complications (haemorrhage, biliary/vascular injury, sepsis) and discharge.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of unstable liver trauma?
back: Surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: In what condition is mesenteric injury most commonly seen?
back: Seat belt syndrome.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a longitudinal tear of the mesentery, and how is it managed?
back: Only one branch of the mesenteric vessel is cut, without loss of vascularity; managed with repair of the tear.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a transverse tear of the mesentery, and how is it managed?
back: All vessels are cut, causing loss of vascularity; managed with resection and anastomosis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the 'lethal triad' of trauma, part of the criteria for damage control surgery (DCS)?
back: Hypothermia (< 34 degrees C), acidosis (pH < 7.2 or serum lactate > 5 mmol/L), and coagulopathy.

subject: Surgery
front: What other criteria (besides the lethal triad) indicate the need for damage control surgery?
back: Blood pressure < 70 mmHg, transfusion approaching 15 units, and injury severity score > 36.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Phase 0 of damage control surgery?
back: Identification of the patient in the emergency room.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Phase 1 of damage control surgery, and its aims?
back: Emergency laparotomy in the operating theatre, aiming to stop bleeding and prevent contamination; ends with temporary abdominal closure.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Phase 2 of damage control surgery, and its aim?
back: ICU care for 24-48 hours, aiming to correct physiology (the 'terrible triad').

subject: Surgery
front: What is Phase 3 of damage control surgery, and its aim?
back: Re-exploration in the operating theatre, aiming to correct anatomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What does 'ETC' (Early Total Care/Extremity/Endpoint of care) refer to in damage control surgery timing?
back: Definitive management should occur within 36 hours of injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What structures are contained in Zone 1 of retroperitoneal trauma, and what is its management?
back: Major vessels; managed with surgery, and carries the maximum mortality.

subject: Surgery
front: What structures are contained in Zone 2 of retroperitoneal trauma?
back: Kidney, ureter, and renal vessels.

subject: Surgery
front: What structures are contained in Zone 3 of retroperitoneal trauma, and what is its management?
back: Pelvic structures; it is the most commonly injured zone, and the majority are managed conservatively.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation of choice for retroperitoneal trauma in stable vs unstable patients?
back: Stable: CECT. Unstable: single-shot IVU.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what are the boundaries/contents of Zone I (central vascular structures) in retroperitoneal trauma, and its exploration techniques?
back: Contains the aorta and IVC; explored via left medial visceral rotation (Mattox manoeuvre) for left-sided structures, or right medial visceral rotation (Kocher's manoeuvre/Cattell-Braasch) for right-sided structures.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, how is Zone II (kidneys and adrenal glands) managed in retroperitoneal trauma, with an exception?
back: Managed with observation, except for an expanding haematoma, which should be explored.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, how is Zone III (pelvic vasculature-associated retroperitoneum) managed?
back: External pelvic compression and fixation.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, how is Zone IV (retrohepatic IVC and hematoma behind the portal vein) managed?
back: Observation.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation of choice for renal trauma in a stable patient?
back: CT urography or CECT.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation of choice for renal trauma in an unstable patient?
back: Single-shot IVP.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation of choice for bladder injury?
back: CT cystography.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation of choice for urethral injury?
back: Retrograde urethrogram (RGU).

subject: Surgery
front: What defines Grade I renal injury (AAST)?
back: Subcapsular haematoma or contusion.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines Grade II renal injury?
back: Superficial laceration <= 1 cm depth, not involving the collecting system; or peri-renal haematoma confined within the fascia.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines Grade III renal injury?
back: Laceration > 1 cm not involving the collecting system; or vascular injury/active bleeding confined within the peri-renal fascia.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines Grade IV renal injury?
back: Laceration involving the collecting system with urinary extravasation; vascular injury to a segmental renal artery or vein; segmental infarction without active bleeding; or active bleeding extending beyond the peri-renal fascia.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines Grade V renal injury?
back: A shattered kidney; or avulsion of the renal hilum/laceration of the main renal artery or vein, causing a devascularised kidney with active bleeding.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a urinoma from Grade IV renal injury managed if it becomes infected, versus if there is no response to conservative management?
back: If infected: pigtail catheter drainage. If no response: DJ stenting. Otherwise, urinoma can be managed conservatively.

subject: Surgery
front: Which is more severe - intraperitoneal or extraperitoneal bladder rupture, and which is more common?
back: Intraperitoneal rupture is more severe (managed with surgery); extraperitoneal rupture is more common (managed conservatively).

subject: Surgery
front: How are most cases of thoracic trauma managed?
back: Conservatively, with chest tubes, since most patients present in a polytrauma setting where this is common.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of death from blunt thoracic trauma?
back: Tracheobronchial injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of death from penetrating thoracic trauma?
back: Pulmonary laceration leading to haemothorax.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of thoracic trauma?
back: Rib fractures.

subject: Surgery
front: Which ribs are most commonly fractured during CPR?
back: The 3rd to 5th ribs.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of simple rib fractures?
back: Analgesia.

subject: Surgery
front: What structures are at risk with a 1st rib fracture, and why?
back: Subclavian vessels, brachial plexus, and the apex of the lung (risk of pneumothorax), due to the high impact force required to fracture the 1st rib.

subject: Surgery
front: What structures are at risk with fractures of the 10th-12th ribs (floating ribs)?
back: The liver (if right-sided) or spleen (if left-sided).

subject: Surgery
front: What defines flail chest?
back: Fracture of 2 or more consecutive ribs at 2 or more places.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of death in flail chest?
back: Pulmonary contusion.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the classic clinical feature of flail chest?
back: Paradoxical chest wall movement - the flail segment moves inward on inspiration and outward on expiration.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the initial management of flail chest, and when is more aggressive management needed?
back: Oxygen plus analgesia; if respiratory rate > 20/min or pO2 < 60 mmHg (hemodynamic compromise), intermittent positive pressure ventilation (IPPV) or surgical fixation is needed.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the pathophysiology of tension pneumothorax?
back: A penetrating injury creates a one-way valve (sucking wound), causing progressive air accumulation, tracheal shift, and cardiac compression.

subject: Surgery
front: What clinical features are seen in tension pneumothorax regarding JVP, breath sounds, and percussion?
back: Increased JVP, decreased/absent breath sounds on the affected side, and a hyperresonant percussion note.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the pathophysiology of hemothorax, and which mechanism is more common - penetrating or blunt?
back: Blood accumulation in the pleural space; penetrating injury is much more common than blunt injury as a cause.

subject: Surgery
front: What clinical features are seen in hemothorax regarding breath sounds and percussion?
back: Decreased breath sounds, and a dull percussion note.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the pathophysiology of cardiac tamponade in trauma?
back: Rapid blood accumulation in the pericardial space.

subject: Surgery
front: What clinical features are seen in cardiac tamponade regarding JVP and heart sounds?
back: Increased JVP, and muffled heart sounds (part of Beck's triad).

subject: Surgery
front: What eFAST (M-mode) findings suggest pneumothorax?
back: Loss of the seashore sign, and presence of the bar code/stratosphere sign.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the emergency management of tension pneumothorax, and the site used in adults vs children?
back: Needle thoracocentesis; adults: 5th intercostal space, midaxillary line. Children: 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the definitive management of tension pneumothorax?
back: Tube thoracocentesis (chest tube insertion).

subject: Surgery
front: How is an open (sucking) chest wound managed?
back: A 3-sided occlusive dressing, creating a flutter valve effect that allows air to escape but not enter.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of cardiac tamponade in trauma, and why is pericardiocentesis not preferred?
back: Emergency left anterolateral thoracotomy, with evacuation of the haematoma and myocardial repair; pericardiocentesis has no role in traumatic cardiac tamponade.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for emergency thoracotomy in chest trauma?
back: More than 1-1.5 litres of blood at chest tube insertion, more than 200 cc/hour of drainage for 3 consecutive hours, cardiac tamponade, tracheobronchial injury, or thoracic aortic injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the boundaries of the 'triangle of safety' for chest tube insertion?
back: Apex: the axilla. Base: the 5th intercostal space. Bordered anteriorly by the anterior axillary line and posteriorly by the mid-axillary line.

subject: Surgery
front: At what level relative to the rib should a chest tube be inserted, and why?
back: At the upper border of the rib, never the lower border, due to the neurovascular bundle running along the lower border.

subject: Surgery
front: How is proper chest tube function assessed?
back: By observing water column movement in the tube with every breath, once connected to an underwater seal bag, and confirmed with a chest X-ray.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the purpose of the water in an underwater seal chest drainage system?
back: To prevent air from being sucked back into the chest during inspiration.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the criteria for removing a chest tube?
back: The lung is fully expanded, output is < 100 cc in 24 hours, and it is removed while the patient holds their breath at the peak of inspiration.

subject: Surgery
front: Which side is more commonly affected by diaphragmatic injury, and why?
back: Left more than right, since the liver protects the right diaphragm.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of diaphragmatic injury?
back: Breathlessness, and bowel sounds heard in the thoracic cavity.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of diaphragmatic injury?
back: Laparotomy, reducing bowel contents back into the abdomen, repairing the diaphragm, and inserting an intercostal (chest) tube.

subject: Surgery
front: Why should blind chest tube insertion be avoided in suspected diaphragmatic injury?
back: Due to risk of bowel injury.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what is the investigation of choice for diaphragmatic injury?
back: Laparoscopy, which is superior to CECT.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what is the Bergqvist triad, associated with diaphragmatic injury?
back: Rib fracture, diaphragm injury, and pelvic/spine fracture.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the boundaries of Zone 1 of neck trauma?
back: From the thoracic inlet to the cricoid cartilage.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the boundaries of Zone 2 of neck trauma, and why is it clinically significant?
back: From the cricoid cartilage to the angle of the mandible; it is the most exposed and surgically accessible zone, and the most commonly injured zone.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the boundaries of Zone 3 of neck trauma?
back: From the angle of the mandible to the base of the skull.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the 'hard signs' of neck trauma requiring surgical exploration?
back: Subcutaneous emphysema, air bubbling from a penetrating wound, expanding neck haematoma, and hoarseness of voice.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what is 'platysma breach,' and what does it necessitate along with the hard signs?
back: Penetration through the platysma muscle; combined with hard signs (expanding/pulsatile hematoma, active bleeding, shock, airway compromise, massive subcutaneous emphysema, neurologic deficit, or Zone 2 injury), it is an indication for surgical exploration.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a non-depressed skull fracture managed?
back: No intervention required.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a depressed skull fracture managed, and what indicates surgery?
back: Surgical elevation is performed if focal neurological signs are present, or if the depth of depression exceeds the depth of adjacent bone structures.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the signs of an anterior cranial fossa (cribriform plate) fracture?
back: Racoon eyes (black eyes), CSF rhinorrhoea and epistaxis (differentiated from normal nasal discharge by the target/halo sign), and anosmia.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the target/halo sign, used to differentiate CSF from blood in nasal or ear discharge?
back: A test using blotting paper, where CSF forms a halo around a central blood spot; CSF is also confirmed by beta-2 transferrin positivity.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the signs of a middle cranial fossa (petrous temporal bone) fracture?
back: Battle sign (discolouration over the mastoid process), haemotympanum, CSF otorrhoea, and facial nerve injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What is paradoxical rhinorrhoea, seen rarely in middle cranial fossa fracture?
back: CSF or fluid collecting in the middle ear, then draining via the Eustachian tube to the nose, rather than the ear.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the signs of a posterior cranial fossa (occipital bone) fracture?
back: Visual problems, occipital contusion, 6th cranial nerve injury, and Vernet/jugular foramen syndrome (injury to the 9th-11th cranial nerves).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for CT head within 1 hour of injury?
back: Loss of consciousness, seizures, more than 1 episode of vomiting, ENT bleed, focal neurological deficit, GCS < 13, GCS < 15 at 2 hours, or open/depressed skull fracture.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for CT head within 8 hours of injury?
back: Age > 65 years, or retrograde amnesia > 30 minutes.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation of choice for head injury overall?
back: NCCT (non-contrast CT).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the primary brain injuries (occurring due to direct impact)?
back: Contusion, concussion, diffuse axonal injury (DAI), extradural haematoma (EDH), and subdural haematoma (SDH).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of intracranial bleed, and its management?
back: Contusion; managed conservatively.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a concussion, and its management?
back: The mildest type of brain injury; managed by avoiding contact sports for a short duration, with no surgical intervention needed.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what is the finding on NCCT in concussion?
back: A normal scan, despite a minor neurological deficit clinically.

subject: Surgery
front: What is secondary brain injury, and what causes it?
back: Injury occurring due to raised intracranial pressure (ICP), secondary to the primary injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the formula for cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP), and its normal value?
back: CPP = MAP minus ICP; normal is > 60 mmHg.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Cushing's reflex, and what triggers it?
back: A response to raised ICP, characterised by hypertension, bradycardia, and altered respiration.

subject: Surgery
front: What age group is typically affected by extradural haematoma (EDH), and its cause?
back: Young patients; caused by high-velocity impact leading to middle meningeal artery rupture.

subject: Surgery
front: What age group is typically affected by chronic subdural haematoma (SDH), and its cause?
back: Elderly patients; caused by trivial injury leading to bridging vein rupture.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the classic clinical feature of EDH?
back: A lucid interval - a period of consciousness between periods of unconsciousness.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the classic clinical course of chronic SDH?
back: Gradual altered sensorium.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the classic imaging appearance of EDH on NCCT, and is it restricted by suture lines?
back: Biconvex/lens-shaped hemorrhage; it is restricted by suture lines.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the classic imaging appearance of chronic SDH on NCCT, and is it restricted by suture lines?
back: Concavo-convex/crescentic hemorrhage; it is not restricted by sutures.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of EDH vs chronic SDH?
back: EDH: craniotomy. Chronic SDH: burr hole or craniotomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What causes diffuse axonal injury (DAI), and in which patients is it seen?
back: High-velocity impact causing shearing force between grey and white matter; can be seen in any trauma patient, and is the most severe type of brain injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the NCCT finding in DAI, and the investigation of choice?
back: NCCT is typically normal; MRI is the investigation of choice, showing punctate hemorrhages at the grey-white matter junction.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the prognosis and management of DAI?
back: Worst prognosis among the primary brain injuries; managed conservatively.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what is Adam's staging used for in DAI, and what are its three stages?
back: Grading the severity of diffuse axonal injury; Stage 1: grey-white matter junction, Stage 2: corpus callosum, Stage 3: brainstem.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what histopathological finding is characteristic of DAI?
back: Retraction balls.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what is the classic clinical feature and site of acute subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH)?
back: The worst headache of the patient's life ('thunderclap headache'/'star of death' appearance on imaging); most commonly caused by trauma more than aneurysm, with the aneurysm's most common site being the ACA-Acom junction.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what is the investigation of choice and gold standard investigation for aneurysmal SAH?
back: CT angiography is the investigation of choice; DSA (digital subtraction angiography) is the gold standard.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what is the treatment of choice for a ruptured aneurysm causing SAH, and when is clipping preferred?
back: Endovascular coiling; clipping is preferred for a wide-neck aneurysm.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what drug prevents vasospasm after SAH, and what is the '3H' therapy used to manage vasospasm?
back: Nimodipine prevents vasospasm; 3H therapy consists of hypervolemia, hemodilution, and hypertension.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what is the Hutchinson pupil, seen in severe head injury?
back: Asymmetric pupil dilation, indicating raised intracranial pressure/uncal herniation.

subject: Surgery
front: According to CoreBTR notes, what is Mount Fuji sign, and what condition does it indicate?
back: A radiological sign of tension pneumocephalus.

subject: Surgery
topic: Head Trauma: Management of Raised ICP & Craniotomy
front: What are the indications for craniotomy in intracranial hemorrhage?
back: Clot size > 30 cc, midline shift > 5 mm, or clot thickness > 1.5 cm.

subject: Surgery
topic: Head Trauma: Management of Raised ICP & Craniotomy
front: What is the preferred surgical site (Pterion) used for craniotomy?
back: The pterion, a thin area of the skull where the frontal, parietal, temporal, and sphenoid bones meet.

subject: Surgery
topic: Head Trauma: Management of Raised ICP & Craniotomy
front: What are the general management principles for raised intracranial pressure (ICP)?
back: Ensure adequate oxygenation and perfusion, avoid hyperglycaemia, administer IV mannitol, and use hyperventilation.

subject: Surgery
topic: Head Trauma: Management of Raised ICP & Craniotomy
front: Are steroids used in head trauma management?
back: No, steroids have no role in head trauma (they are used in brain tumours, not trauma).

subject: Surgery
topic: Head Trauma: Management of Raised ICP & Craniotomy
front: What are the treatment goals for managing raised ICP?
back: CPP >= 60 mmHg, serum sodium 135-145 mEq/L, SBP >= 100 mmHg, and blood glucose 80-180 mg/dL.

subject: Surgery
topic: Head Trauma: Management of Raised ICP & Craniotomy
front: What is the Glasgow Outcome Score used for, and what do scores 1 through 5 represent?
back: A prognostic score after head injury; 1: death, 2: persistent vegetative state, 3: severe disability, 4: moderate disability, 5: good recovery.

subject: Surgery
front: What components make up the Revised Trauma Score (RTS)?
back: Systolic BP, respiratory rate, and GCS.

subject: Surgery
front: What components make up the Mangled Extremity Severity Score (MESS)?
back: Type of injury (energy level), presence of shock, presence of ischemia signs, and age group.

subject: Surgery
front: What MESS score indicates a salvageable limb, versus a score indicating amputation is necessary?
back: Score <= 6: limb salvageable. Score >= 7: amputation necessary.

subject: Surgery
front: In the MESS energy group, what points are given for low, medium, high energy, and massive crush injuries?
back: Low energy (stab wound, simple closed fracture, small-calibre GSW): 1 point. Medium energy (open/multilevel fracture, dislocation, moderate crush): 2 points. High energy (shotgun, high-velocity GSW): 3 points. Massive crush (logging, railroad, oil rig accidents): 4 points.

subject: Surgery
front: In the MESS shock group, what points are given for normotensive, transiently hypotensive, and prolonged hypotensive states?
back: Normotensive (BP stable): 0 points. Transiently hypotensive (unstable in field, responsive to fluid): 1 point. Prolonged hypotension (SBP < 90 mmHg in field, responsive to IV fluids in OT): 2 points.

subject: Surgery
front: In the MESS ischemia group, what points are given for none, mild, moderate, and advanced ischemia?
back: None (pulsatile, no ischemia signs): 1 point. Mild (diminished pulses, no ischemia signs): 2 points. Moderate (no Dopplerable pulse, sluggish cap refill, paraesthesia, diminished motor activity): 3 points. Advanced (pulseless, cool, paralyzed, numb, no cap refill): 4 points.

subject: Surgery
front: In the MESS age group, what points are given for age <30, 30-50, and >50 years?
back: <30 years: 0 points. 30-50 years: 1 point. >50 years: 2 points.

subject: Surgery
front: What GCS score defines brain death?
back: GCS = 3.

subject: Surgery
front: How is GCS scored in an intubated patient?
back: Denoted as 'VNT' (verbal not testable).

subject: Surgery
front: What characterizes decorticate posturing, and where is the lesion located?
back: Flexion of the upper limbs; lesion is above the midbrain, with the rubrospinal tract active.

subject: Surgery
front: What characterizes decerebrate posturing, and where is the lesion located?
back: Extension of the upper limbs; lesion is below the midbrain, with the rubrospinal tract inactive.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the criteria for referral to a specialised burns unit?
back: Burns involving the face, hands, feet, genitalia, perineum, or major joints; chemical burns; electrical burns; inhalational injury; partial thickness burns > 10% TBSA; and any third-degree (full thickness) burns.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the initial management approach for a burns patient?
back: ABCDE - airway, breathing, circulation, disability, exposure.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the signs of airway burns?
back: Burnt/singed nasal hair (most specific sign), hoarseness of voice, carbonaceous deposits in sputum, closed room burns, burns involving the head/face/neck, and altered mental sensorium.

subject: Surgery
front: When is prophylactic intubation indicated in burns patients?
back: If any signs of airway burns are present, to prevent airway collapse.

subject: Surgery
front: What breathing complications occur in burns patients?
back: Hypoxia, CO poisoning, and eschar formation around the chest (managed with escharotomy).

subject: Surgery
front: What causes dehydration/hypovolemic shock in burns patients (circulation)?
back: An inflammatory response causing vasodilation and leaky capillaries.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines localised vs generalised burns based on TBSA, and their clinical significance?
back: Localised: burns < 10% TBSA. Generalised: burns > 10% TBSA, which can lead to hypovolemic shock.

subject: Surgery
front: How is the total calculated fluid volume divided across the first 24 hours in burns resuscitation?
back: First half given over the first 8 hours; the next half given over the following 16 hours.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the old Parkland's formula for burns fluid resuscitation?
back: 4 mL x body weight (kg) x %TBSA burned.

subject: Surgery
front: What formula is used for paediatric burns fluid resuscitation?
back: Galveston formula.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the adjusted fluid rate for adults and older children (>= 14 years) with flame or scald burns, and expected urine output?
back: 2 mL Ringer's lactate x kg x %TBSA; urine output goal of 0.5 mL/kg/hr.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the adjusted fluid rate for children (<= 14 years) with flame or scald burns?
back: 3 mL Ringer's lactate x kg x %TBSA.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the adjusted fluid rate for infants/young children (<=30 kg) with flame or scald burns, and expected urine output?
back: 3 mL Ringer's lactate x kg x %TBSA, plus a sugar-containing solution at maintenance rate; urine output goal of 1 mL/kg/hr.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the adjusted fluid rate for electrical injuries (with myoglobinuria), and expected urine output?
back: 4 mL Ringer's lactate x kg x %TBSA, continued until urine clears; urine output goal of 1-1.5 mL/kg/hr until urine clears.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most commonly used colloid resuscitation formula for burns, and when is albumin given?
back: Muir and Barclay formula; albumin (protein) is given after the first 12 hours, to decrease the risk of oedema.

subject: Surgery
front: What methods are used to calculate total body surface area (TBSA) burned?
back: Wallace rule of 9, and the Lund and Browder chart (considered the best/most accurate method).

subject: Surgery
front: What layer of skin is involved in first-degree burns, and its appearance?
back: Only the epidermis; appears red and tender.

subject: Surgery
front: What layers are involved in superficial second-degree burns, and their appearance?
back: Epidermis and papillary dermis; appears red and tender with blister formation.

subject: Surgery
front: What layers are involved in deep second-degree burns, and their appearance?
back: The entire epidermis and dermis; appears red and less tender.

subject: Surgery
front: What layers are involved in third and fourth-degree burns, and their appearance?
back: Third degree involves subcutaneous tissue; fourth degree involves muscle; appears black and charred, and is painless.

subject: Surgery
front: How does blanching differ across burn degrees?
back: Present in first-degree and superficial second-degree burns; present in some areas only in deep second-degree burns; absent in third/fourth-degree burns.

subject: Surgery
front: How do first-degree burns heal?
back: Without scarring, in 3-5 days.

subject: Surgery
front: How do superficial second-degree burns heal?
back: Without scarring, after application of dressing materials.

subject: Surgery
front: How do deep second-degree burns heal?
back: With hypertrophic scars and keloids.

subject: Surgery
front: How are third and fourth-degree burns managed?
back: Early excision followed by split thickness skin grafting (STSG).

subject: Surgery
front: With what should a burn wound be washed initially?
back: Room temperature water.

subject: Surgery
front: Should burn blisters be burst?
back: No, blisters should not be burst.

subject: Surgery
front: What injection routes should be avoided in burns patients, and why?
back: IM and SC injections, due to unpredictable absorption from damaged tissue.

subject: Surgery
front: When should an NG tube be inserted in burns patients, and why?
back: If burns exceed 15% TBSA, due to increased risk of ileus.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the caloric requirement for severe burns, compared to normal?
back: 40 kcal/kg/day, approximately double the normal requirement.

subject: Surgery
front: During what time period is nitrogen loss maximal in burns patients?

subject: Surgery
front: What proportion of calories should come from protein in burns patients, and what formula calculates protein requirement?
back: At least 20% of calories; calculated using the Davies formula.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the purpose of escharotomy, and what complication does it prevent?
back: To prevent eschar from causing compartment syndrome and difficulty breathing.

subject: Surgery
front: What layer is cut during escharotomy, and how far should the incision extend?
back: The deep fascia is cut; the wound should extend beyond the deep burn.

subject: Surgery
front: How is bleeding managed during and after escharotomy?
back: Significant bleeding vessels are controlled with diathermy; a hemostatic dressing is applied post-operatively.

subject: Surgery
front: What dressing is used for first-degree burns?
back: The wound is simply exposed.

subject: Surgery
front: What dressings are used for second-degree burns?
back: Vaseline/paraffin gauze, collagen dressing, or hydrocolloid dressing.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most commonly used topical agent for burns, and its antimicrobial coverage?
back: Silver sulfadiazine; effective against Pseudomonas and gram-negative bacteria.

subject: Surgery
front: What is silver nitrate used for in burns, and its limitation?
back: Effective against Pseudomonas, but has decreased action against gram-negative bacteria.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the features of mafenide acetate in burns management?
back: Painful application, penetrates eschar well, but causes metabolic acidosis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is considered the best topical agent for burns?
back: Cerium nitrate.

subject: Surgery
topic: Burns: Causes of Death & Special Situations
front: What is the most common overall cause of death in burns, and the most common causative organism?
back: Septic shock; the most common organism is Pseudomonas.

subject: Surgery
topic: Burns: Causes of Death & Special Situations
front: What is the most common cause of immediate death in burns?
back: Asphyxia, more common than neurogenic shock.

subject: Surgery
topic: Burns: Causes of Death & Special Situations
front: What is the most common cause of death in the early phase (1-3 days) of burns?
back: Hypovolemic shock.

subject: Surgery
topic: Burns: Causes of Death & Special Situations
front: What is the most common cause of death in the late phase (>3 days) of burns?
back: Septic shock.

subject: Surgery
topic: Burns: Causes of Death & Special Situations
front: Between acid and alkali burns, which is more severe, and why?
back: Alkali burns are more severe, causing liquefactive necrosis with deeper penetration.

subject: Surgery
topic: Burns: Causes of Death & Special Situations
front: Should chemical burns ever be neutralised, and what is the correct management?
back: Never neutralise; wash with water and brush off any chemical powder.

subject: Surgery
topic: Burns: Causes of Death & Special Situations
front: What are the clinical features of hydrofluoric acid burns?
back: Increased serum potassium, decreased serum calcium, and arrhythmias.

subject: Surgery
topic: Burns: Causes of Death & Special Situations
front: What is the management of hydrofluoric acid burns?
back: Calcium gluconate.

subject: Surgery
topic: Burns: Causes of Death & Special Situations
front: What degree of burns do electrical burns typically cause, and what complications are associated with alternating current exposure?
back: High-degree (3rd/4th degree) burns; alternating current causes myoglobinuria and arrhythmias.

subject: Surgery
topic: Burns: Causes of Death & Special Situations
front: What should be examined in electrical burns, and what is the management?
back: Both entry and exit burn wounds; managed with the old Parkland's formula for fluids, plus early excision and STSG.

subject: Surgery
topic: Burns: Causes of Death & Special Situations
front: What is a filigree burn, seen in lightning injury?
back: A superficial burn pattern seen in exposed areas, resulting from an indirect lightning strike.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines a simple/uncomplicated reducible hernia vs an irreducible hernia?
back: Reducible hernia: contents can be pushed back; irreducible hernia: contents cannot be pushed back, but there is no obstruction or strangulation.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines an obstructed hernia, and is cough impulse present?
back: An irreducible hernia with bowel obstruction; cough impulse is absent, and forceful taxis (reduction) may be contraindicated due to risk of reduction en masse.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines a strangulated hernia?
back: An obstructed hernia with compromised blood supply and inflamed overlying skin.

subject: Surgery
front: What is an omentocele, and what are its examination features?
back: A hernia containing omentum; has a doughy consistency, no peristalsis, is difficult to reduce initially, and gives a dull percussion note.

subject: Surgery
front: What is an enterocele, and what are its examination features?
back: A hernia containing bowel; shows peristalsis, is easy to reduce initially, and gives a tympanic percussion note.

subject: Surgery
front: What is an Amyand hernia?
back: A hernia containing the appendix.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a Littre hernia?
back: A hernia containing a Meckel's diverticulum.

subject: Surgery
front: What is herniotomy, and what does it involve?
back: Identifying the sac and cutting it open to reduce the contents; the defect itself is not repaired.

subject: Surgery
front: What is herniorrhaphy, and what does it involve?
back: Herniotomy followed by suturing the edges of the defect together.

subject: Surgery
front: What is hernioplasty, and what does it involve?
back: Herniotomy followed by closing the defect with a mesh.

subject: Surgery
front: How do herniotomy, herniorrhaphy, and hernioplasty compare in terms of recurrence rate?
back: Herniotomy has the highest recurrence rate; hernioplasty has the least recurrence rate.

subject: Surgery
front: In which situations is herniotomy the treatment of choice?
back: Congenital inguinal hernia, inguinal hernia in children, and congenital hydrocele.

subject: Surgery
front: What surgery is used for an infected and strangulated hernia?
back: Herniotomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What features characterize the best mesh material for hernia repair?
back: Low weight (leading to less shrinkage), thin fibres, and large pores.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of mesh used, and its limitation?
back: Synthetic mesh (e.g. Prolene); avoided in the presence of infection.

subject: Surgery
front: What is biological mesh used for, and its main disadvantage?
back: Can be used in the presence of infection; its main disadvantage is high cost.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of hernia overall, and the most common type of inguinal hernia?
back: Inguinal hernia is the most common hernia overall; indirect inguinal hernia is the most common type of inguinal hernia.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of hernia in females?
back: Femoral hernia.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the single best clinical test for inguinal hernia?
back: Deep ring occlusion test.

subject: Surgery
front: What other clinical tests are used for inguinal hernia, besides the deep ring occlusion test?
back: Zieman's three-finger test, and the ring invagination test.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the boundaries of Hesselbach's triangle?
back: Lateral: inferior epigastric vessels. Medial: rectus abdominis. Inferior: inguinal ligament.

subject: Surgery
front: How does a direct inguinal hernia relate to Hesselbach's triangle, compared to an indirect hernia?
back: Direct hernia passes through Hesselbach's triangle; indirect hernia passes lateral to Hesselbach's triangle.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the boundaries of the myopectineal orifice of Fruchaud?
back: Superior: arching fibres of the internal oblique. Inferior: pectineal/Cooper's ligament. Lateral: tendon of the iliopsoas. Medial: outer border of the rectus abdominis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgical significance of the myopectineal orifice of Fruchaud?
back: Mesh placement covering this orifice can repair defects of inguinal, femoral, and obturator hernias simultaneously.

subject: Surgery
topic: Inguinal Hernia Surgery: Complications & Nerve Injury
front: What are the complications of open inguinal hernia surgery?
back: Haemorrhage, injury to the vas deferens/cord structures, nerve injury, recurrence, and wound infection.

subject: Surgery
topic: Inguinal Hernia Surgery: Complications & Nerve Injury
front: Which nerve is most commonly injured at the superficial ring during open inguinal hernia surgery?
back: The ilioinguinal nerve.

subject: Surgery
topic: Inguinal Hernia Surgery: Complications & Nerve Injury
front: Which nerve, if entrapped below the mesh during inguinal hernia repair, causes chronic pain?
back: The iliohypogastric nerve.

subject: Surgery
topic: Inguinal Hernia Surgery: Complications & Nerve Injury
front: According to CoreBTR notes, injury to which nerve causes loss of sensation over the suprapubic region after hernia surgery?
back: The iliohypogastric nerve.

subject: Surgery
topic: Inguinal Hernia Surgery: Complications & Nerve Injury
front: According to CoreBTR notes, injury to which nerve causes loss of sensation over the root of the penis after hernia surgery?
back: The ilioinguinal nerve.

subject: Surgery
topic: Inguinal Hernia Surgery: Complications & Nerve Injury
front: According to CoreBTR notes, injury to which nerve causes loss of the cremasteric reflex after hernia surgery, and from which spinal roots does it arise?
back: The genitofemoral nerve, arising from L1 and L2 spinal roots.

subject: Surgery
topic: Inguinal Hernia Surgery: Complications & Nerve Injury
front: According to CoreBTR notes, which nerve injury causes loss of sensation over the lateral thigh, and in what type of hernia surgery is it most commonly injured?
back: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh (LCNT, also known as LCFN); it is most commonly injured during laparoscopic hernia surgery.

subject: Surgery
topic: Inguinal Hernia Surgery: Complications & Nerve Injury
front: What is meralgia paraesthetica, and what causes it during laparoscopic hernia surgery?
